analogy: Think of "Climate" through the chapter's own opening images: CLIMATE AND VEGETATION This unit deals with • Weather and climate – spatial and temporal distribution of temperature, pressure, winds and rainfall; Indian monsoons: mechanism, onset and variability – spatial and temporal; climatic types • Natural vegetation – forest types and distribution; wild life conservation;... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: CLIMATE AND VEGETATION, 28 INDIA : PHYSICAL ENVIRONMENT, UNITY AND DIVERSITY IN THE MONSOON CLIMATE, CLIMATE. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Climate is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that cLIMATE AND VEGETATION This unit deals with • Weather and climate – spatial and temporal distribution of temperature, pressure, winds and rainfall; Indian monsoons: mechanism, onset and variability – spatial and temporal; climatic types • Natural vegetation – forest types and distribution; wild life conservation; biosphere reserves UNIT III 2026-27

The text explains that 28 INDIA : PHYSICAL ENVIRONMENT We drink more water during summers.

The text explains that your uniform during the summer is different from the winters.

The text explains that why do you wear lighter clothes during summers and heavy woollen clothes during winters in north India?

The text explains that in southern India, woollen clothes are not required.

The text explains that in northeastern states, winters are mild except in the hills.

The text explains that there are variations in weather conditions during different seasons.

The text explains that these changes occur due to the changes in the elements of weather (temperature, pressure, wind direction and velocity, humidity and precipitation, etc.).

The text explains that weather is the momentary state of the atmosphere while climate refers to the average of the weather conditions over a longer period of time.

The text explains that weather changes quickly, may be within a day or week but climate changes imperceptively and may be noted after 50 years or even more.

The text explains that you have already studied about the monsoon in your earlier classes.

The text explains that you are also aware of the meaning of the word, “monsoon”.

The text explains that monsoon connotes the climate associated with seasonal reversal in the direction of winds.

The text explains that india has hot monsoonal climate which is the prevalent climate in south and southeast Asia.

The text explains that uNITY AND DIVERSITY IN THE MONSOON CLIMATE The monsoon regime emphasises the unity of India with the rest of southeast Asian region.

The text explains that this view of broad unity of the monsoon type of climate should not, however, lead one to ignore its regional variations which differentiate the weather and climate of different regions of India.

The text explains that the climate of India has many regional variations expressed in the pattern of winds, temperature and rainfall, rhythm of seasons and the degree of wetness or dryness.

The text explains that let us take a closer look at these regional variations in temperature, winds and rainfall.

The text explains that churu in Rajasthan may record a temperature of 50°°C or more on a June day while the mercury hardly touches 19°°C in Tawang (Arunachal Pradesh) on the same day.

The text explains that on a December night, temperature in Drass (Ladakh) may drop down to minus 45°°C while Thiruvananthapuram or Chennai on the same night records 20°°C or 22°°C.

The text explains that these examples confirm that there are seasonal variations in temperature from place to place and from region to region in India.

The text explains that not only this, if we take only a single place and record the temperature for just one day, variations are no less striking.

The text explains that in Kerala and in the Andaman Islands, the difference between day and night temperatures may be hardly seven or eight degree Celsius.

The text explains that but in the Thar desert, if the day temperature is around 50°°C, at night, it may drop down considerably upto 15°°-20°°C.

The text explains that you also know that the Tropic of Cancer passes through the central part of India in east-west direction.

The text explains that the tropical zone being nearer to the equator, experiences high temperatures throughout the year with small daily and annual range.

The text explains that area north of the Tropic of Cancer being away from the equator, experiences extreme climate with high daily and annual range of temperature.

The text explains that this differential heating of land and sea creates different air pressure zones in different seasons in and around the Indian subcontinent.

The text explains that difference in air pressure causes reversal in the direction of monsoon winds.

The text explains that areas in the interior of India are far away from the moderating influence of the sea.

The text explains that that is why, the people of Mumbai and the Konkan coast have hardly any idea of extremes of temperature and the seasonal rhythm of weather.

The text explains that on the other hand, the seasonal contrasts in weather at places in the interior of the country such as Delhi, Kanpur and Amritsar affect the entire sphere of life.

The text explains that altitude : Temperature decreases with height.

The text explains that for example, Agra and Darjiling are l o c a t e d o n t h e s a m e l a t i t u d e , b u t temperature of January in Agra is 16°°C whereas it is only 4°°C in Darjiling.

The text explains that relief : The physiography or relief of India also affects the temperature, air pressure, direction and speed of wind and the amount and distribution of rainfall.

The text explains that many attempts have been made to discover the exact nature and causation of monsoon, but so far, no single theory has been able to explain the monsoon fully.

The text explains that systematic studies of the causes of rainfall in the South Asian region help to understand the causes and salient features of the monsoon, particularly some of its important aspects, such as: (i) The onset of the monsoon.

The text explains that this causes the formation of an intense low pressure in the northwestern part of the subcontinent.

The text explains that since the pressure in the Indian Ocean in the south of the landmass is high as water gets heated slowly, the low pressure cell attracts the southeast trades across the Equator.

The text explains that inter Tropical Convergence Zone (ITCZ) The Inter Tropical Convergence Zone (ITCZ) is a low pressure zone located at the equator where trade winds converge, and so, it is a zone where air tends to ascend.

The text explains that figure 4.1 : Onset of Monsoon Inter Tropical Convergence Zone (ITCZ) The Inter Tropical Convergence Zone (ITCZ) is a low pressure zone located at the equator where trade winds converge, and so, it is a zone where air tends to ascend.

The text explains that these breaks in the different regions are due to different reasons: (i) In northern India rains are likely to fail if the rain-bearing storms are not very frequent along the monsoon trough or the ITCZ over this region.

The text explains that the Cold Weather Season Temperature : Usually, the cold weather season sets in by mid-November in northern India.

The text explains that the mean daily temperature remains below 21°°C over most parts of northern India.

The text explains that the night temperature may be quite low, sometimes going below freezing point in Punjab and Rajasthan.

The text explains that there are three main reasons for the excessive cold in north India during this season : (i) States like Punjab, Haryana and Rajasthan being far away from the moderating influence of sea experience continental climate.

The text explains that (ii) The snowfall in the nearby Himalayan ranges creates cold wave situation; and (iii) Around February, the cold winds coming from the Caspian Sea and Turkmenistan EI-Nino and the Indian Monsoon EI-Nino is a complex weather system that appears once every three to seven years, bringing drought, floods and other weather extremes to different parts of the world.

The text explains that the system involves oceanic and atmospheric phenomena with the appearance of warm currents off the coast of Peru in the Eastern Pacific and affects weather in many places including India.

The text explains that this current increases the temperature of water on the Peruvian coast by 10°°C.

The text explains that (a) High pressure develops over North-western India due to low temperatures.

The text explains that (b) Low pressure develops over North-western India due to high temperatures.

The text explains that (c) No changes in temperature and pressure occur in north-western India.

Source: NCERT Class 11 India Physical Environment, Chapter 4: Climate
